coredata.dc:title: A method for synthesis of oriented epitope-imprinted open-mouthed polymer nanocapsules and their use for fluorescent sensing of target protein
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shiting
Liu, Zhiqiang
Jin, Siyu
Bai, Yufei
Feng, Xingjia
Fu, Guoqi
coredata.dc:description: Epitope imprinting has proved to be an effective way for fabricating artificial receptors for protein recognition. Surface imprinting over sacrificial supports is particularly favorable for generating high-quality epitope-imprinted cavities, but obtaining nanomaterials by this way is still a challenge. Herein, we propose a method for the synthesis of oriented surface epitope-imprinted open-mouthed polymer nanocapsules (OM-MIP NCs) by sacrificing asymmetric template-modified Janus nanocores. Amine/aldehyde functionalized SiO2 Janus nanoparticles were prepared via the molten-wax-in-water Pickering emulsion approach, an easy scale-up technique. Epitope templates and vinyl groups were coupled to the aldehyde-bearing major side, whereas polyethylene glycol (PEG) chains were grafted to the amine-modified side. Incomplete imprinted shells were then generated principally on the non-PEGylated side via aqueous precipitation polymerization, hence affording OM-MIP NCs after etching the SiO2 nanocores. With a C-terminus nonapeptide of bovine serum albumin (BSA) chosen as a model epitope and polymerizable carbon dots added to the pre-polymerization solution, fluorescent OM-MIP NCs were synthesized for sensing of BSA. Such NCs reached maximal fluorescent response within 15 min, greatly faster than the closed imprinted NCs within 130 min, proving good accessibility of their inner-surface imprinted cavities thanks to the open mouths. Furthermore, they showed excellent target protein detection performance, with an imprinting factor of 7.8, a limit of detection of 43.8 nM and a linear range of 0.2–6 μM. The recoveries in bovine serum samples at four spiking levels ranged from 99.2 to 107.2%, with relative standard deviations of 1.2–5.9%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Epitope imprinting
Surface imprinting
Nanoscale imprinting
Oriented surface imprinting
Fluorescent detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of epitope, vinyl and PEG multi-functionalized Janus nanoparticles
Synthesis of fluorescent open-mouthed epitope-imprinted polymer nanocapsules
Synthesis and characterization of fluorescent open-mouthed epitope-imprinted polymer nanocapsules

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21674051 ) and the National Key R&D Program of China ( 2017YFC11044001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A), (B) SEM images of the SiO2–NH2 NPs coated wax spheres at two magnifications. (C) Photographs showing the locations of SiO2–CHO NPs (a), SiO2–NH2 NPs (b) and SiO2–CHO//NH2 JNPs (c) added to a dual-phase system of dichloromethane and water. (D) Zeta potentials of pristine SiO2 NPs (a), SiO2–NH2 NPs (b), SiO2–CHO NPs (c), SiO2–CHO//NH2 JNPs (d), SiO2-epitope//NH2 JNPs (e), SiO2-epitope/vinyl//NH2 JNPs (f), SiO2-epitope/vinyl//PEG JNPs (g), OM-MIP NCs (h) and OM-NIP NCs (i) in the PB buffer.

(A) TG curves of SiO2–NH2 NPs (a), SiO2-PEG NPs (b), SiO2-epitope/vinyl NPs (c), SiO2-epitope/vinyl//PEG JNPs (d), SiO2-viny//PEG JNPs (e), and the corresponding nanostructures (f)–(j) obtained after the imprinting polymerization over (a)–(e), respectively. (B) TEM images of SiO2 NPs (a), and the corresponding nanostructures obtained after imprinting polymerization over SiO2-PEG NPs (b), SiO2-epitope/vinyl//PEG JNPs (c) and SiO2-vinyl//PEG JNPs (d), respectively. All the scale bars represent 50 nm.

(a) The hydrodynamic diameters of OM-MIP NCs (black line) and OM-NIP NCs (red line) measured in the PB buffer at 25 °C. The insets are the TEM images of OM-MIP NCs (above) and OM-NIP NCs (below). (b) Transmittance of OM-MIP NCs in the PB buffer with increasing temperatures. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The absorption spectrum of BSA (a), and the fluorescence emission spectra of the OM-MIP NCs in the absence of (b) and in the presence of (c) BSA (1 μM) and GMA-CDs (d) at λ ex = 346 nm.
Comparison of the fluorescence response kinetics of OM-MIP and OM-NIP NCs (a) with that of closed MIP and NIP NCs (b) to BSA (1 μM).

Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) OM-MIP and (b) OM-NIP NCs in the presence of BSA with increasing concentrations (0–6 μM), respectively. The insets show the corresponding Stern–Volmer plots with the estimated K SV as well as R 2. (c) Fluorescence enhancement of OM-MIP and OM-NIP NCs together with the OM-MIP NCs synthesized using the epitope of HSA as the template in the presence of different proteins (1 μM). (d) Fluorescence response of the OM-MIP NCs to BSA (1 μM) mixed with HSA of multifold higher concentrations.
Schematic illustration of the procedure for synthesizing fluorescent oriented surface epitope-imprinted polymer nanocapsules with an open mouth.

head.ce:title: A method for synthesis of oriented epitope-imprinted open-mouthed polymer nanocapsules and their use for fluorescent sensing of target protein
head.ce:dedication: Dedicated to the 100th anniversary of Chemistry at Nankai University.

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Polymer Materials of Ministry of Education, Institute of Polymer Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Nankai University, Tianjin, 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Polymer Materials of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Polymer Materials of Ministry of Education, Institute of Polymer Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Nankai University, Tianjin, 300071, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Epitope imprinting has proved to be an effective way for fabricating artificial receptors for protein recognition. Surface imprinting over sacrificial supports is particularly favorable for generating high-quality epitope-imprinted cavities, but obtaining nanomaterials by this way is still a challenge. Herein, we propose a method for the synthesis of oriented surface epitope-imprinted open-mouthed polymer nanocapsules (OM-MIP NCs) by sacrificing asymmetric template-modified Janus nanocores. Amine/aldehyde functionalized SiO2 Janus nanoparticles were prepared via the molten-wax-in-water Pickering emulsion approach, an easy scale-up technique. Epitope templates and vinyl groups were coupled to the aldehyde-bearing major side, whereas polyethylene glycol (PEG) chains were grafted to the amine-modified side. Incomplete imprinted shells were then generated principally on the non-PEGylated side via aqueous precipitation polymerization, hence affording OM-MIP NCs after etching the SiO2 nanocores. With a C-terminus nonapeptide of bovine serum albumin (BSA) chosen as a model epitope and polymerizable carbon dots added to the pre-polymerization solution, fluorescent OM-MIP NCs were synthesized for sensing of BSA. Such NCs reached maximal fluorescent response within 15 min, greatly faster than the closed imprinted NCs within 130 min, proving good accessibility of their inner-surface imprinted cavities thanks to the open mouths. Furthermore, they showed excellent target protein detection performance, with an imprinting factor of 7.8, a limit of detection of 43.8 nM and a linear range of 0.2–6 μM. The recoveries in bovine serum samples at four spiking levels ranged from 99.2 to 107.2%, with relative standard deviations of 1.2–5.9%.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular imprinting is a technique towards artificial synthesis of polymer receptors, termed molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), which outperform the natural receptors due to their high stability, ease of production and low cost. MIPs are usually synthesized by firstly crosslinking polymerization of functional monomers in the presence of template molecules. After template removal, specific cavities complementary to the templates are generated in the resultant polymer matrix. MIPs have been widely used in a variety of fields, such as affinity separation media, molecular recognition elements for sensors, and specific target-capturing materials in ligand binding assays [1]. Compared with the imprinting of low-molecular-weight compounds, the imprinting against proteins and other biomacromolecules is still challenged by their unique characters such as large size, complex structure and variable conformation [2–4]. Therefore, a variety of approaches have been developed, such as surface imprinting [5–9], nanoscale imprinting [10–12], solid synthesis [13,14] and epitope imprinting [15,16].

For antigen-antibody recognition in nature, an antibody interacts with only a small part of the antigen, i.e. antigenic site which is termed as epitope. As epitope imprinting is concerned, an exposed amino acid sequence, glycan or monosaccharide of a target protein, instead of the whole biomacromolecule, is usually used as the templates for imprinting [16]. Compared with proteins, in particular rare proteins, these templates are not only more easily obtained by artificial synthesis, but also much more robust during the imprinting process. Till now, epitope-imprinted materials have been used to recognize peptides and cells in addition to target proteins. Therefore, as important alternatives to antibodies, epitope imprinting has shown potential applications in various areas, such as disease biomarker detection, proteomic sample pretreatment, recombinant protein purification, biosensors, cancer diagnosis and therapy [16–21].

The methods for the synthesis of epitope-imprinted materials mainly include surface imprinting [15,22–29], inverse microemulsion polymerization [30,31] and solid synthesis [32,33]. Among these, surface imprinting is widely employed since it usually involves relatively easy procedures. The strategies for surface epitope imprinting can be classified into two categories: retention of the support materials (RSM) [23–26] and sacrificing of the support materials (SSM) [15,22,27–29]. For the RSM strategy, templates are either directly dissolved in the pre-polymerization solutions or immobilized on the surface of support materials (e.g. nanoparticles or slide glass), and then surface-grafted polymerization is carried out, affording epitope-imprinted materials with core-shell or double-layer structures. With free epitopes, the imprinting process is easily carried out; however, the imprinted cavities are heterogeneous, and also template utilization efficiency is low since only the imprinted cavities approaching the surface of the imprinted polymer layers can contribute to target recognition [23]. If epitopes are oriented surface-immobilized, homogeneous imprinted cavities can be formed with high-efficacy recognition performance, and also the template molecules can be fully utilized; but the thickness of the imprinted polymer layers should be precisely controlled to match the length of the epitopes [24–26]. As for the SSM strategy, till now three approaches have been proposed based on different sacrificial support materials from which epitopes are orientedly anchored, i.e. polymerization within macroporous SiO2 spheres (also known as hierarchical imprinting method) [27,28], surface polymerization on glass slides [15,22], and polymerization of SiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) stabilized Pickering emulsions [29].

Generally speaking, the remarkable advantages of the SSM-based approach lies in that, the polymerization need not to be controlled rigorously, but only need proceed sufficiently, and hence high-quality oriented imprinted cavities can be formed much more easily, in comparison with the RSM way. However, only epitope imprinted spheres (tens to hundreds of μm in size) [27–29] or flat films [15,22] have been obtained with the SSM strategy described above, but nano-sized imprinted materials with high surface-to-volume ratio have not been reported. The nanoscale imprinted materials are urgently needed in the applications such as biosensors, drug delivery and cell imaging. Moreover, as the major SSM approach by sacrificing macroporous SiO2 spheres [27,28], the resultant mirror-structured porous imprinted polymer spheres undoubtedly exhibit significantly hindered target rebinding, since the recognition cavities are generated inside the pore channels.

Herein, we demonstrate a novel SSM-based method for the synthesis of open-mouthed epitope-imprinted polymer nanocapsules (OM-MIP NCs) for overcoming the limitations of the existing SSM approaches. The molten-wax-based o/w Pickering emulsion technique was employed to prepare SiO2 Janus nanoparticles (JNPs), with the major part of the surface immobilized with both epitope templates and vinyl groups for subsequent surface epitope imprinting, and the remaining part grafted with polyethleneglycol (PEG) chains for prevention from local surface polymerization. Using the as-functionalized JNPs as the nanosupports, surface imprinting was carried out via aqueous precipitation polymerization, with crosslinked polymer shells principally formed over the epitope/vinyl anchored major surface of the JNPs. After etching the SiO2 nanocores and removing the templates, the OM-MIP NCs were obtained, with epitope-imprinted cavities arrayed orientedly on the inner surface and an open mouth for the macromolecular target to freely transfer into the NCs. Meanwhile, polymerizable carbon dots (CDs) were added to the pre-polymerization solution for endowing the OM-MIP NCs with photo-luminescence, so that their recognition performance could be facilely evaluated by fluorescent detection.

The following reagents were used for fabrication of the sensors: tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS) and aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) (Aladdin, China); glycidyl methacrylate (GMA), acrylic acid, ethanediamine, triethylamine and ammonium persulfate (APS, recrystallized before use) (Tianjin Chemical Reagents Co., China); glutaraldehyde, paraffin wax (melting point: 50–52 °C), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), acrylamide (AAm), N,N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA), N,N,N′,N′-tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED), cytochrome c (Cyt c), trypsin, bovine serum albumin (BSA) and lysozyme (Lyz) (Sangon, China); human serum albumin (HSA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), ovalbumin (OVA) and trypsin (Sigma-Aldrich, China); fetal bovine serum (GIBCO, USA); N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAm, recrystallized from hexane before use) (Acros Organics, Belgium); N-tert-butylacrylamide (TBAm) (Alfa Aesar, China); two nonapeptides VVSTQTALA and AASQAALGL (GL Biochem, China); methoxy polyethylene glycol succinimidyl carbonate (denoted as mPEG-NHS) with an average molecular weight of 2000 (Huateng Pharma, China); 4-acrylamidophenyl (amino)methaniminium acetate (AB) synthesized according to the reference method [33]. All the other chemicals were used as received unless otherwise stated. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure deionized water (>18.6 MΩcm) produced by a UPR-II purification system (Chengdu YouPu Biotech Co., China). Sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.0, 10 mM) and 0.1 M sodium phosphate buffer + 0.15 M NaCl (pH 7.4) were denoted as PB and PBS, respectively.

The vinyl functionalized CDs were synthesized according to the literature method [34]. Briefly, the CDs with amino and carboxyl groups on their surface were synthesized by a simple microwave-assisted pyrolysis process using acrylic acid and ethylenediamine as the carbon source and surface passivation agent, respectively. GMA was used as a vinylation agent for reaction with the amine groups on the CDs’ surface, affording polymerizable CDs (denoted as GMA-CDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of epitope, vinyl and PEG multi-functionalized Janus nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, SiO2 NPs were synthesized by the Stöber method [35]. For amine modification, dry SiO2 NPs (1.0 g) were dispersed in anhydrous toluene (40 mL) containing APTES (0.8 mL), and then stirred at 30 °C for 8 h, followed by centrifugation and washing with ethanol and water. The resultant amine-functionalized NPs were denoted as SiO2–NH2.

Aldehyde/amine asymmetrically functionalized Janus SiO2 NPs (named as SiO2–CHO//NH2 JNPs) were prepared via the molten-wax-in-water Pickering emulsion approach using SiO2–NH2 NPs proposed by Granick and co-workers [36]. SiO2–NH2 NPs (400 mg) were dispersed in water (40 mL). After adding wax (4 g), the dispersion was heated to 80 °C for melting the wax and mechanically stirred at 1500 rpm for 1 h, and then cooled to room temperature, resulting in SiO2–NH2 NPs coated wax spheres. For aldehyde modification of the exposed side of the SiO2–NH2 NPs, the SiO2/wax colloidosomes were incubated in the PBS containing 7.5% glutaraldehyde for 8 h, followed by washing with water and drying. The resultant SiO2–CHO//NH2 JNPs were released from the colloidosomes by dissolving the wax with chloroform. For comparison, aldehyde modified SiO2 NPs (denoted as SiO2–CHO NPs) was prepared by direct incubating SiO2–NH2 NPs in the same glutaraldehyde solution.

For immobilization of epitope templates and vinyl groups to the SiO2–CHO//NH2 JNPs, the JNPs were incubated in 16 mL of the PBS containing 8 mg of VVSTQTALA at room temperature for 6 h, followed by incubation for further 6 h after adding allylamine hydrochloride (60 mg, 0.64 mmol) to the dispersion. Subsequently, these JNPs were washed with the PBS and dispersed in the PBS containing NaBH4 (1 mg/mL) for 30 min at room temperature. Finally, the resultant JNPs (denoted as SiO2-epitope/vinyl//NH2 JNPs) were washed with water. Additionally, the SiO2–CHO//NH2 JNPs modified with only allylamine but without the epitope templates were also obtained in the similar way, and resultant JNPs were denoted as SiO2-vinyl//NH2 JNPs. The SiO2–NH2 NPs immobilized with both epitope template and vinyl groups or only vinyl groups using glutaraldehyde as a coupling agent were also performed in the similar way, and the resultant NPs were denoted as SiO2-epitope/vinyl NPs and SiO2-vinyl NPs, respectively.

For PEGylation of the amine-bearing side of the above obtained SiO2-epitope/vinyl//NH2 JNPs, they were incubated in acetonitrile (20 mL) containing mPEG-NHS (40 mg, 0.02 mmol) and triethylamine (20 mg, 0.2 mmol) at 40 °C under argon atmosphere for 12 h. The final JNPs (denoted as SiO2-epitope/vinyl//PEG JNPs) were washed with ethanol and dried for further use. Additionally, the SiO2-vinyl//NH2 JNPs and SiO2–NH2 NPs were PEGylated in the same way and the resultant products were denoted as SiO2-vinyl//PEG JNPs and SiO2-PEG NPs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent open-mouthed epitope-imprinted polymer nanocapsules
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, surface-imprinted polymer nanoshells were formed over the SiO2-epitope/vinyl//PEG JNPs. For this purpose, the JNPs (50 mg) were incubated in 0.8 mL of the PB buffer containing AB (1.54 mg, 0.002 mmol) at room temperature for 30 min, allowing for the interaction of the C-terminus carboxyl groups of the immobilized peptide with the basic amidine moiety of AB [33]. After washing out the excessive AB, the JNPs were mixed in 5 mL of the PB buffered pre-polymerization solution of NIPAm (125.5 mg, 1.112 mmol), TBAm (25.5 mg, 0.168 mmol), AAm (2.4 mg, 0.032 mmol), MBA (42.1 mg, 0.328 mmol) and GMA-CDs (16 mg) with a total monomer concentration of 0.5% (w/v). After purging with nitrogen for 15 min, 0.05 mL of 2% (w/v) SDS solution, 0.05 mL of 2% (w/v) APS solution and 0.05 mL of 0.25% (v/v) TEMED solution were added to the suspension subsequently and precipitation polymerization was continued under magnetic stirring at 37 °C for 15 h. Afterwards, the resulting core-shell structures were incubated in NH4HF2 solution (0.125 M) for 1 h to partially etched the inside SiO2 nanocores to dissociate the epitope template originally anchored on the SiO2 nanocores, followed by washing with CH3OH:acetic acid (9:1) for removal of the templates from the imprinted polymer shells and final neutralization with CH3OH: 16% ammonia water (3:7) for dissociating the carboxyl/amidine complex. The remaining SiO2 parts were then completely etched with NH4HF2 solution (6 M) for 3 h, and the residual polymer suspension was dialyzed against water for 48 h and then freeze-dried, affording open-mouthed epitope-imprinted polymer nanocapsules (OM-MIP NCs). The non-imprinted polymer controls (OM-NIP NCs) were synthesized in the same way but using the SiO2-vinyl//PEG JNPs as the nanosupports for the precipitation polymerization.

For comparison, epitope-imprinted and non-imprinted polymer nanocapsules (denoted as MIP NCs and NIP NCs, respectively) without open mouths were also synthesized via the same procedure but using the SiO2-epitope/vinyl and SiO2-vinyl NPs as the nanosupports for the precipitation polymerization, respectively.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a fluorospectrophotometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Japan), with the slit widths of excitation and emission set at 10 and 20 nm, respectively, and the photomultiplier tube voltage set at 700 V. All the samples (10 μg/mL) for fluorescence measurements were dispersed by ultrasonication for several seconds with an ultrasonic cleaner (SB-3200 DTD, Ningbo SCIENTZ Biotech Co., China) in the PB buffer, unless stated otherwise. UV–Vis absorption was measured on a spectrophotometer (TU-1900, Purkinje General Instrument Co., China) equipped with a temperature controller. The morphologies and structures were observed by a TEM (Talos F200X G2, FEI, Netherlands) and a SEM (Apreo S LoVac, Thermoscientific, USA). Zeta potentials and hydrodynamic diameters were measured by DLS (Zeta Sizer Nano-ZS90, Malvern, UK). TGA was performed by a thermogravimetric analyzer (TG 209, NETZSCH, Germany) under nitrogen atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C/min up to 800 °C.

Fetal bovine serum was employed to examine the practicability and accuracy of the imprinted NCs for target protein detection. Briefly, the bovine serum was diluted 1000-fold with the PB buffer. The imprinted NCs (10 μg) were added into the diluted bovine serum solution (1 mL) spiked with the target protein at four concentration levels. The fluorescence spectra of the dispersion were recorded and all the tests were performed in triplicates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of fluorescent open-mouthed epitope-imprinted polymer nanocapsules
ce:section.ce:para: Pickering emulsion method is highly favorable to elaborate Janus colloidal particles with controllable surface properties and, moreover, can be readily scaled up to produce larger amount of particles [37,38]. Thus, this method was employed to synthesize anisotropic epitope-functionalized SiO2 NPs as the sacrificial nanosupports for the construction of OM-MIP NCs. The procedure is shown in Scheme 1 . SiO2–NH2 NPs were partially embedded in (hence protected by) solidified wax spheres while cooling down these NPs-stabilized molten-wax-in-water emulsion. After modifying the exposed part of the SiO2–NH2 NPs pointing towards the outer aqueous phase with glutaraldehyde and then dissolving the wax, SiO2–CHO//NH2 JNPs were obtained. Herein, the C-terminus nonapeptide (VVSTQTALA) of BSA was chosen as a model epitope template to prepare OM-MIP NCs for BSA recognition. The SiO2–CHO//NH2 JNPs were firstly modified with the peptide through formation of Schiff bases between its N-terminus amine group and the surface aldehydes of the JNPs, followed by blocking the residual aldehyde groups with allylamine (hence introducing vinyl groups) and reducing the Schiff bases with NaBH4. Then PEG chains were attached via the nucleophilic reaction of the surface amine groups with mPEG-NHS, hence affording SiO2-epitope/vinyl//PEG JNPs. The as-prepared JNPs, after incubated in an AB monomer solution for the formation of strong interactions between the amidine moiety and the C-terminus carboxylate group of the immobilized peptide [33], were employed as the nanosupports for surface imprinting via aqueous precipitation copolymerization of NIPAm, TBAm, AAm, MBA and GMA-CDs. Fluorescent cross-linked polymer shells were principally formed over the epitope/vinyl modified major side of the JNPs, since the PEG-grafted side could prevent from local surface polymerization. Therefore, fluorescent oriented epitope-imprinted nanocapsules with an open mouth, so-called OM-MIP NCs, were obtained, after dissolving the SiO2-based nanocores and removal of the peptide template.

The pristine SiO2 NPs were synthesized via the Stöber method [35] with a rather uniform size of about 210 nm (supporting information, Fig. S1a). As shown in Fig. 1a and b , by the molten-wax-in-water emulsion Pickering emulsion approach, SiO2–NH2 NPs coated wax spheres of about 10–30 μm in size were obtained, with the NPs embedded into the wax up to about one third of their diameter. Thus, partial surface modification of the SiO2–NH2 NPs with glutaraldehyde was achieved, resulting in SiO2–CHO//NH2 JNPs. This was proved by the different location of these JNPs from that of SiO2–CHO and SiO2– NH2 NPs added in a dual-phase mixture of dichloromethane and water [39]. As shown in Fig. 1c, the hydrophilic SiO2–CHO NPs dispersed well in the top water phase and the slightly hydrophobic SiO2–NH2 NPs in the bottom dichloromethane phase, while the SiO2–CHO//NH2 JNPs like molecular surfactants were located at the two-phase interface due to the disparity in the hydrophilic and hydrophobic features of the two sides. The zeta potential of the SiO2–CHO//NH2 JNPs was measured to be between that of SiO2–CHO and SiO2– NH2 NPs (see Fig. 1d), also suggesting their Janus surface characteristics.

The TG curves of different NPs and JNPs along with the corresponding core-shell structures after the imprinting polymerization over them were measured, as shown in Fig. 2 A. According to the residual weight percentage of the SiO2–NH2 and SiO2-PEG NPs at 800 °C (w a and w b, respectively), the weight increase percentage (%Δw) of the SiO2–NH2 NPs after the reaction with mPEG-NHS was calculated to be 2.75% (w a/w b - 1), i.e. ~27.5 mg of mPEG per gram of the SiO2–NH2 NPs, corresponding to a grafting density of 0.63 PEG chains/nm2 estimated based on a SiO2 density of 2.2 g/cm3. After the imprinting polymerization over the SiO2–NH2 and SiO2-PEG NPs, the residual weight percentage of resultant core-shell nanostructures decreased to w f and w g due to the formation of pyrolyzable polymer shells, and the %Δw relative to the nanocores were estimated to be 7.86% (w a/w f - 1) and 0.71% (w b/w g - 1), respectively. This meant that, compared with the amine-exposed surface, the PEG-grafted surface could nearly prevent from the imprinting polymerization. This can be explained by assuming that, unlike the surface-anchored amine groups, the highly hydrophilic PEG chains with no strong H-bond donor cannot form relatively strong hydrogen bonds with the monomers like NIPAm, AAm and MBA in the imprinting polymerization system. Furthermore, the %Δw of the obtained core-shell particles after the imprinting polymerization over the SiO2-epitope/vinyl//PEG JNPs and SiO2-vinyl//PEG JNPs was estimated to be 10.20% and 10.25%, respectively, about half that over the SiO2-epitope/vinyl NPs (20.19%). This proved the polymerization prevention efficacy of the partially PEG-grafted JNPs, just like the wholly PEGylated NPs described above. As shown in the TEM images (see Fig. 2B), like the bare SiO2 NPs, no polymer layer can be discriminated from the SiO2-PEG NPs after the imprinting polymerization. The polymer layers of the SiO2-PEG NPs also could not be observed due to the relatively low PEG grafting amount (data not shown). Whereas, after the polymerization, the polymer shells can be distinguished clearly over both the SiO2-epitope/vinyl//PEG JNPs and SiO2-vinyl//PEG JNPs.

Dissolving the inorganic cores from the above core-shell nanostructures encapsulating the SiO2-epitope/vinyl//PEG JNPs or SiO2-vinyl//PEG JNPs, followed by template removal, should yield open-mouthed imprinted and non-imprinted polymer nanocapsules, so-called OM-MIP and OM-NIP NCs, respectively. Regrettably, the open mouths could not be observed by either TEM (see Fig. 3 a) or SEM (data not shown), since the soft hydrogel-based NCs collapsed and shrank while freeze-dried. The hydrodynamic diameters of the OM-MIP and OM-NIP NCs were measured by DLS to be about 311 and 305 nm, respectively, significantly larger than the sizes of the dried samples observed by TEM, as seen from Fig. 3a. These NCs were nearly neutrally-charged, quite different from the functionalized SiO2 NPs or JNPs (see Fig. 1D). As can be seen from Fig. 3b, the NCs showed obvious thermos-responsiveness with a volume phase transition temperature (VPTT) of about 34 °C, because the major component in the pre-polymerization solution was NIPAm (65 mol%), a monomer used for the formation of a typical temperature-sensitive polymer, polyNIPAm. Since the above hydrodynamic diameters were measured at 25 °C, below the VPTT, the OM-MIP and OM-NIP NCs were in a swollen state and hence remarkably larger than the corresponding dried samples.

Polymerizable carbon dots (GMA-CDs) were added to the imprinting polymerization solution for combining the specific recognition of the epitope-imprinted NCs with the fluorescence sensing property of photo-luminescence CDs. The GMA-CDs showed a size less than 2 nm (supporting information, Fig. S1b), and illustrated relatively wide fluorescence excitation and emission spectra (supporting information, Fig. S2). The CDs embedded OM-MIP and OM-NIP NCs showed stable fluorescence emission for up to 3 h, hence enough for fluorescence measurements, and an optimized λ ex = 346 nm was chosen for the following investigation (supporting information, Fig. S3).

As shown from Fig. 4 , the OM-MIP NCs showed rather strong emission peaks at λ em of 392 nm, with a slight hypsochromic shift compared with the emission spectrum of GMA-CDs. It should be noted that, OM-MIP NCs exhibited fluorescence enhancement in the presence of the target protein, BSA. Therefore, such molecular imprinting based fluorescence (MI-FL) sensors are a “turn-on” type, which is beneficial for sensitive and selective detection with less interference by the background fluorescence, compared with the “turn-off” type [40]. On the other hand, the fluorescence emission spectra of the OM-MIP NCs did not overlap with the absorption spectrum of BSA, so that the fluorescence response could be ascribed to the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between the CDs and the protein [40].

The fluorescence enhancement is quantified by F/F0 - 1, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of an analyst, respectively. The ratio of F/F0 - 1 for a MIP relative to that for the corresponding NIP, i.e. relative fluorescence enhancement denoted as (F/F0 - 1)MIP/NIP, can be employed to evaluate the fluorescence response of the MIP due to the specific binding to the imprinted cavities. Based on (F/F0 - 1)MIP/NIP, the conditions for the synthesis of fluorescent OM-MIP NCs, such as the mass ratio of GMA-CDs relative to the nanocores and total monomer concentration, were optimized (supporting information, Figs. S4a and b). Additionally, the temperature and pH value employed for fluorescent detection of protein was optimized to be 25 °C and pH 7, respectively (supporting information, Figs. S4c and d).

The epitope-imprinted cavities can be generated in both the OM-MIP NCs and the closed MIP NCs. For examining the effect of the OM-MIP NCs’ open mouths on their target protein recognition performance, the closed epitope-imprinted and non-imprinted polymer NCs (termed MIP and NIP NCs) were prepared the same way as the OM-MIP and OM-NIP NCs, except for using the SiO2-epitope/vinyl and SiO2-vinyl NPs as the nanocores, respectively. The fluorescence response kinetics of the two kinds of epitope-imprinted and non-imprinted NCs to BSA binding was measured, respectively, as shown in Fig. 5 . Amazingly, there was great disparity in the time required for achieving the stable fluorescence intensities, with ~15 min for the open-mouthed NCs vs. ~130 min for the closed NCs. This can be explained by that the protein molecules could rapidly enter the open-mouthed NCs though the mouths and then access the imprinted cavities inside, in contrast to slow diffusion into the closed NCs though the cross-linked polymer wall. This further confirmed the existing of the open mouths on the OM-MIP and OM-NIP NCs. Their fluorescence response time is compared to that for the QDs-based surface imprinted nanomaterials reported previously [41,42], suggesting that the imprinted cavities even on the inner surface of the NCs did not constitute an obstacle to access of the target protein thanks to the open mouths.

For testing the ability for sensitive target protein detection, the fluorescence spectra of both the OM-MIP and OM-NIP NCs were measured in the presence of BSA with the concentrations ranging from 0 to 6 μM. As shown in Fig. 6a and b , both kinds of NCs illustrated fluorescence enhancement response to BSA binding, but the enhancing extent for the OM-MIP NCs were remarkably larger than that for the corresponding OM-NIP NCs, because of the epitope-imprinted cavities generated on the inner surface of the OM-MIP NCs. The quantitative fluorescence enhancement (F/F0 − 1), as defined above, should follow the Stern−Volmer equation
(1)
F/F 0
- 1 =
K sv
C
where K SV is the Stern−Volmer constant, and C is the concentration of the target protein [42,43]. The ratio of K SV,MIP to K SV,NIP is defined as the Stern−Volmer equation based imprinting factor (IFSV). In the studied BSA concentration range, F/F0 − 1 for both OM-MIP and OM-NIP NCs was well fitted to the Stern−Volmer equation, with the correlation coefficients achieving 0.99. The K sv values were estimated to be 0.674 and 0.087 μM−1 for the OM-MIP and OM-NIP NCs, respectively, hence with the IFSV for the OM-NIP NPs figured to be 7.75. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ) for the OM-MIP NCs were estimated according to 3σ/s [44–47] and 10σ/s [48], respectively, where σ means the standard deviation of the blank measurements (n = 10) and s means the slope of calibration curve (herein, s = K sv). The LOD and LOQ were determined to be 43.8 nM and 145.9 nM, respectively. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) were 3.1, 0.8 and 1.5% for 0.2, 1 and 4 μM of BSA, respectively, based on three measurements. Considering both IFSV and LOD, the OM-NIP NCs synthesized in this work showed more outstanding detection performance, compared with other molecular imprinting based fluorescence sensors for BSA or HSA detection reported previously (supporting information, Table S1). Moreover, the OM-MIP NCs demonstrated much lower LOD and higher IFSV than the closed MIP NCs (supporting information, Fig. S5), probably due to relatively poor accessibility to the imprinted cavities inside the closed NCs.

The recognition selectivity of the OM-MIP NCs for the target protein, i.e. BSA, was examined by comparing their fluorescent response toward BSA with that toward other reference proteins having different Mw and isoelectric point (pI) under a same concentration (1 μM). The Mw and pI all the proteins used are BSA (Mw 67 k, pI 4.8), HSA (Mw 66 k, pI 4.8), OVA (Mw 44.5 k, pI 4.5), Lyz (Mw 14 k, pI 11.1), Cyt c (, Mw 12.4 k, pI 10.2), trypsin (Mw 24 k, pI 10.5). As shown in Fig. 6c, the (F/F0 - 1)MIP/NIP for the reference proteins was about 0.45–1.76, noticeably lower than 6.1 for BSA, because of the presence of the cavities imprinted with its C-terminus nonapeptides as the epitope. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of the OM-MIP NCs toward BSA (1 μM) was almost not affected by mixing with HSA (up to five-fold higher concentration), a homologous protein with the similar amino acid sequence, Mw and pI (Fig. 6d). For further enhancement of the sensing selectivity, a ratiometric fluorescence system may be constructed by introduction of a reference fluorescence substance which is not affected by analyte [40,44].

To preliminarily examine the generality of the proposed method for the synthesis of epitope-imprinted OM NCs, we also used the C-terminus nonapeptide (AASQAALGL) of HSA as an epitope template to prepare MIP NCs, termed OM-MIP NCs (HSA epitope), for HSA recognition. As can be seen from Fig. 6c, the resultant imprinted NCs also exhibited selective recognition of HSA.

The potential application of the OM-MIP NCs as fluorescence sensors was further investigated in diluted bovine serum samples spiked with different BSA concentrations. The bovine serum was 1000-fold diluted so that the BSA concentration was within the linear range of the calibration curve obtained above. Satisfactory recoveries and accuracies were achieved with a recovery range of 99.2–107.2% and RSDs of 1.2–5.9% for the spiked samples with four concentrations (supporting information, Table S2). Therefore, the OM-MIP NCs may be practically used for accurate and sensitive detection of low BSA concentrations in real samples.

In summary, we have demonstrated a method for the synthesis of oriented surface epitope-imprinted open-mouthed polymer NCs for target protein recognition. This was achieved by scarifying SiO2-epitope/vinyl//PEG JNPs after the creation of incomplete imprinted polymer shells over them via aqueous precipitation polymerization. The molten-wax-in-water Pickering emulsion technique provided an effective way for the synthesis of the anisotropically functionalized SiO2 JNPs. The locally PEGylated surface of the JNPs could prevent from the polymerization, hence responsible for the formation of the open mouths on the resultant imprinted NCs and ensuring good accessibility of their inner-surface imprinted cavities to the target protein. The resulting carbon dots embedded BSA-epitope-imprinted NCs exhibited satisfactory performance for fluorescent detection of BSA.

As nanosized artificial antibodies with high surface-to-volume-ratio, such kind of NCs are featured with both oriented inner-surface imprinted cavities and an open mouth which facilitates biomacromolecule transfer. They may potentially be applied to construction of other biosensors such as SPR and QCM. In this work, the oriented immobilization of the BSA or HSA epitope was achieved through the only one amine group at the N-terminus based on glutaraldehyde coupling chemistry. However, if an epitope sequence bears multiple amine groups, more general immobilization strategy has to be explored. Work on this direction is already in progress and will be communicated in due course.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21674051) and the National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFC11044001).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2021.122690.

Shiting Zhang: performed most of the experiments and wrote the manuscript. Zhiqiang Liu: helped Shiting Zhang to solve several problems during the investigation. Siyu Jin: synthesized the GMA-CDs and often discussed with Shiting Zhang during the investigation. Yufei Bai: preliminarily synthesized the SiO2–CHO//NH2 JNPs. Xingjia Feng: synthesized the AB monomer. Guoqi Fu: as the supervisor of the above graduates, proposed the idea and revised the manuscript.
